channel.title: Engineering on Carlos A. Planchón
channel.description: Recent content in Engineering on Carlos A. Planchón

item.title: Memory Management in Rust: Ownership and Borrowing Explained
item.description:
Rust achieves memory safety without a garbage collector through its ownership system. This article explores the core concepts that make this possible.

The Problem

Traditional memory management approaches have trade-offs:

  • Garbage collectors add runtime overhead
  • Manual memory management is error-prone (buffer overflows, use-after-free, double-free)

Rust solves this at compile time through its ownership system.

Ownership Rules

Rust has three fundamental ownership rules:

  1. Each value has a single owner
  2. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped
  3. There can only be one mutable reference OR multiple immutable references
fn main() {
    let s1 = String::from("hello");  // s1 owns the String
    let s2 = s1;                      // ownership moves to s2
    // println!("{}", s1);            // ERROR: s1 no longer valid
    println!("{}", s2);               // Works fine
}

Borrowing

Borrowing allows references to data without taking ownership:

item.title: Principles of Operation of Industrial Sensors
item.description:
Industrial sensors are the foundation of modern automation and process control. They convert physical phenomena into electrical signals that can be measured, monitored, and used to control systems. This post explores the operating principles of the most common sensor types.

Temperature Sensors

Thermocouples

Based on the Seebeck effect: when two dissimilar metals are joined, a voltage proportional to temperature difference develops between the junction and the open ends. They offer wide temperature range (-200°C to 2300°C) but lower accuracy.

item.title: Building a Personal Blog with Hugo
item.description:
This is a test post to see how the grid layout handles three items. Hugo is a fantastic static site generator that allows you to build fast, secure websites.

Why Hugo?

Hugo is known for being incredibly fast. It can build a site in milliseconds, which makes it perfect for blogs.

hugo new site my-blog
cd my-blog
hugo server -D
